title: Overview
subtitle: TIMBER | PEAT | 3 PERSON TEAM
description: This cutover bog had been drained, the peat was compacted and Rushes and scrub were spreading. Much of the peat had already been lost. A detailed Restoration Action Plan was developed using information from drone and ground surveys. Data was collected on peat depth, slope analysis, drain depth and width and the vegetation mapped. Planned works were screened and approved by NPWS before work began. Restoration works included reprofiling peat banks, installing dams, removing conifers, and stabilising bare peat. The site is now on a path to recovery. Rewetting will slow carbon loss, improve habitats for wildlife, and encourage the return of peat-forming plants. Recovery takes time but it has begun.

title: Waste Management
subtitle: TIMBER | PEAT | 3 PERSON TEAM
description: Responsible waste management is essential during restoration. All waste must be identified and managed in line with the local Waste Management Plan. Waste materials include both materials dumped on the bog and waste generated by the team during restoration works. On this site, waste included plastic bags used for gathering turf, a water tank, tyres, pallets, and packaging. Larger items were removed, using machinery where necessary. Litter was collected by hand, both were brought to the roadside to enable proper disposal. Waste materials were sorted for recycling. Careful waste management protects wildlife, safeguards water quality, and ensures that good conservation work is not undermined.

title: Logistics and Safety for Blanket Bog Restoration
subtitle: TIMBER | PEAT | 3 PERSON TEAM
description: Successful restoration depends on careful planning, safe working, and respect for fragile ground. Materials such as timber, stone, geotextiles, and coir rolls sourced in advance, stored close to the site and kept dry until needed. When work began, they were carefully transported to minimise surface damage. Staff received safety briefings and were issued suitable footwear, gloves, high-visibility vests, and hard hats. Hazards were identified and marked and lone working restricted to monitoring tasks. Clear emergency plans ensured that everyone knew how to get in and out safely. Good planning of logistics protected both the team and the bog, giving restoration the best chance to succeed.

title: Timber Dams
subtitle: TIMBER | PEAT | 3 PERSON TEAM
description: Shallow drains once carried water quickly off this bog, drying the peat and favouring Rushes over Mosses. Timber dams were installed to slow this flow and re-wet the bog. Built from untreated larch planks, the dams are keyed into the drain banks and embedded deeply into the peat to form a tight seal. Stainless steel screws fix the planks to upright posts driven into the channel bed. A small notch directs water safely over the dam, with timber or stone below to prevent erosion from falling water. These pools raise the local water table, keeping more of the bog wet. Wet conditions favour Sphagnum moss and will reduce Rush dominance. The planks are expected to last 8-10 years. By then, vegetation and organic matter will have filled the drains, blocking them naturally.

title: Peat Plugs
subtitle: TIMBER | PEAT | 3 PERSON TEAM
description: Large drains dug deep into the peat have lowered the water table and accelerated the breakdown of peat. Restoration needs this process to be reversed, and the peat itself is the perfect material for this. Peat is dug from nearby borrow pits, packed firmly into the drain, and keyed into the banks. The original surface vegetation is replaced on top. Each plug is built slightly higher than the surrounding bog to allow for natural settling. Peat plugs work best where a thick peat layer remains at the base. The water level behind each dam will rise, removing the gradient that allowed water to drain from the bog. When properly installed, peat plugs can last indefinitely, allowing drains to naturally disappear as the bog heals.

title: Composite Wood-Peat Dams
subtitle: TIMBER | PEAT | 3 PERSON TEAM
description: Composite wood-peat dams provide extra strength where peak water flows could place a lot of pressure on dams. This could happen where the drain is wide (1.5-2.5 metres), deep, (1m) or on sloping ground. Heavy horizontal larch planks are keyed into the banks; these are reinforced with strong timber posts driven deep into the base of the drain to form a sturdy frame. In wet conditions these timbers will last for more than 30 years. A compacted peat plug is placed upslope of the frame and topped with the original surface vegetation. This combines the strength of timber with the natural sealing properties of peat. Composite dams are a durable solution where added stability is essential to support for long-term restoration.

title: Stone Dams
subtitle: TIMBER | PEAT | 3 PERSON TEAM
description: Stone dams are used where little peat remains or where the base of the drain is down to the mineral layer. In these conditions, peat or timber dams could fail as water might pass underneath. Built from non-calcareous stone such as granite or sandstone. The use of Limestone is avoided to protect the bogs delicate water chemistry. Stone dams slow water flow preventing further erosion. They trap sediment and encourage organic matter to accumulate behind them. This further slows the flow of water, stopping further erosion and protecting the remaining peat.

title: Geotextiles for Peat Stabilisation
subtitle: TIMBER | PEAT | 3 PERSON TEAM
description: Bare peat is vulnerable to erosion and breakdown through contact with the air. On exposed areas, biodegradable jute netting is laid over the surface and secured with wooden pins. The netting shields the peat from heavy rain, reducing erosion and preventing peat particles from washing away. This is especially important on reprofiled peat banks where vegetation has been largely removed. Geotextiles help retain surface moisture during dry periods, creating better conditions for mosses and seedlings to establish. Over time, plants grow through the mesh and bind the surface together. The jute gradually decomposes and becomes incorporated into the peat. Geotextiles provide temporary support at a critical stage, protecting fragile peat surfaces and accelerating the return of healthy bog vegetation.

title: Removing Self-Sown Conifers
subtitle: HAND TOOLS | SAW | LOPPERS | TWO PERSON TEAM
description: Seeds of Sitka spruce from nearby plantations had begun to colonise the bog. Conifers cast shade, limiting the light for Sphagnum moss and other bog plants. They also draw water from the peat and lower the water table. Their branches provide perches for predators such as Grey Crows, increasing the risks for ground-nesting birds like Meadow Pipit, Snipe, Curlew and Hen Harrier. Seedlings are pulled by hand. Larger saplings cut with loppers or saws and left on the ground to decay. Removing self-sown conifers restores open, wet conditions, protects wildlife, and supports the recovery of peat-forming vegetation. It is a simple but vital step in bringing the bog back to life.

title: Reprofiling Peat Banks
subtitle: HEAVY MACHINERY | PEAT | LOGISTICS TEAM
description: Historic turf cutting left steep peat banks, known as hags. Though they appear stable, their exposed faces dry out, crack, and leak carbon to the atmosphere. Dry peat decays rapidly. Heather often grows on top, but this is just hiding the ongoing damage rather than preventing it. To restore these areas, steep faces are graded into gentle slopes. Where large bare areas remain, biodegradable geotextiles may be used to reduce erosion and support plant establishment. Once vegetation recovers, less peat is exposed to the air, decay slows, water is retained more effectively, and the bog begins to stabilise. Reprofiling protects vulnerable peat and helps secure the bogs long-term recovery.

title: Coir Logs
subtitle: COIR LOGS | TIMBER POSTS | MACHINERY | 3 PERSON TEAM
description: Coir is the husk from Coconuts. Packed into rolls or logs, they are often used in peatland restoration. They can be used to block gullies, dam small drain or as simple bunds in Rush dominated areas. The Coir logs will slow the flow of water and raise the local water table. Over time we expect the rushes to decline and mosses to become more important. Coir is biodegradable but lasts long enough to slow water flow, encourage sediment to accumulate and the growth of vegetation. The coir will last for three to five years, by this time its role will have been taken over by the plants that establish behind it.
